planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring in moist, fertile, well-drained to average soil in full sun to part shade; plume poppy spreads vigorously by rhizomes and can be invasive in ideal conditions — plant only where spreading is welcome or where it can be contained by a barrier. The milky, orange-yellow sap can irritate sensitive skin. In rich, moist soil it may exceed 10 feet in height.

directions: Plant in moist to average, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; plume poppy is a majestic, large-scale perennial from China and Japan that creates an extraordinary tropical effect in the temperate garden. The enormous, deeply scalloped, fig-leaf-shaped leaves — each up to 12 inches across — are carried alternately along tall, glaucous, blue-gray stems and have striking bluish-gray surfaces with contrasting creamy-white undersides that flash pale when the wind moves through them. In mid to late summer, the plant produces enormous, branching plumes of hundreds of tiny, petal-less, buff-cream to pink flowers on stems reaching 6-10 feet, creating an airy, feathery mass that floats above the bold foliage below. It spreads freely by rhizomes and can colonize a large area quickly, but this vigor makes it invaluable for large-scale screening and rapid ground coverage in difficult areas. A single plant creates a dramatic focal point of scale and presence rarely achieved by any other hardy perennial. The orange-yellow sap is irritating to skin; handle with gloves.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring or plant container-grown plants in moist to wet, fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun to part shade; yellow meadow rue is adapted to moist conditions including wet meadows and stream margins. The subspecies glaucum (Spanish Meadow Rue) has particularly glaucous, blue-green foliage and is slightly more drought-tolerant than the species.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately wet, fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun to part shade; yellow meadow rue is a tall, elegant European perennial distinguished by its outstanding glaucous (blue-green) foliage — the divided, finely cut, columbine-like leaves are a particularly intense, powdery, blue-green color that provides striking cool contrast in the summer garden independent of the flowers. In early to midsummer, the plant produces fluffy, branching clusters of numerous small, lemon-yellow, petal-less flowers at the top of tall, upright stems — the fluffy effect comes from the prominent, crowded yellow stamens that form the visible flower structure, as the true petals have been reduced to small sepals. Native to moist meadows and streambanks throughout Europe, it is adapted to consistently moist conditions and succeeds beside water features, in bog gardens, and in moist, partly shaded borders. The subspecies glaucum from Spain and Portugal has particularly intensely glaucous foliage and is more drought-tolerant than the typical species. Excellent as a tall filler in the moist or partly shaded border.

planting.note: Sow seeds on the surface of well-drained, lean, neutral to slightly alkaline soil (do not cover — seeds need light) in early spring or early fall; nettle-leaved mullein is a genuine long-lived perennial, unlike the common biennial mullein, returning reliably year after year. It self-sows moderately to maintain a colony. The cultivar Album has white flowers with the same mauve-purple stamens and is equally ornamental.

directions: Sow seeds on the soil surface in lean, very well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; nettle-leaved mullein is the most reliably perennial and garden-worthy of the mulleins, distinct from both the common biennial mullein (Verbascum thapsus) and the silvery Turkish mullein (V. bombyciferum) in being a genuine long-lived perennial that returns each year. The flower spikes reach 3-5 feet and are densely packed with yellow flowers, but the most distinctive and ornamental feature is the prominent, woolly, mauve-purple filaments of the stamens visible in the center of each flower — a striking purple-and-yellow bicolor combination visible across the garden. The cultivar Album produces white flowers with the same contrasting purple-mauve stamens — an unusual and beautiful combination. Native to central and southern Europe, it thrives in the lean, well-drained, alkaline soils of traditional cottage-style dry borders, rock gardens, and gravel plantings. Combines beautifully with Mediterranean herbs, silver-leafed plants, and ornamental grasses.

planting.note: Sow seeds in moist, fertile, well-drained to average soil in full sun to part shade; pokeweed establishes from seed and spreads readily by bird-dispersed seeds. CAUTION: all parts of this plant are toxic to humans and livestock — the roots, berries, and seeds especially so. Wear gloves when handling the plant. Despite being a dangerous plant to ingest, it is extremely valuable as a wildlife plant and is best managed in a wilder area of the garden.

directions: Sow seeds in moist, fertile, well-drained to average soil in full sun to part shade; American pokeweed is a large, dramatic native perennial of eastern North America with striking ornamental qualities — brilliant magenta-pink hollow stems, large tropical-looking leaves, long white flower racemes, and most notably the clusters of deep purple-black, shiny berries in late summer and fall that are among the most important food sources for migrating thrushes, mockingbirds, woodpeckers, and other fall-migrating birds that disperse the seeds widely. Despite being a generous wildlife resource, the entire plant — especially the roots, berries, and seeds — is dangerously toxic to humans and livestock, containing phytolaccatoxin and phytolaccigenin that cause severe gastrointestinal symptoms. The magenta pigment in the berries (pokeweed ink) was historically used to write documents, including some drafts of the US Declaration of Independence. The young spring shoots (poke sallet) have been boiled and eaten in Appalachian tradition after multiple changes of water to reduce toxins, but this practice is risky and not recommended. Best established in naturalized areas.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in spring or late summer in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to part shade; Buchanan's sage is damaged below about 25 degrees F and is best suited to zones 8-10. In zone 7 it can survive mild winters in sheltered positions. The flowers are very large for a salvia — 2 inches or more — and appear primarily in summer through fall. Hummingbirds are strongly attracted to the deep color and nectar.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to part shade; Buchanan's sage is a Mexican native producing some of the darkest, most intensely colored flowers in the genus — the very large, 2-inch tubular flowers are a deep, rich, ruby-magenta to dark cherry-red with a distinctive velvety sheen and a texture from the dense hairs on both corolla and calyx that makes the flowers appear almost jewel-like. Unusually large for a salvia flower, the individual blooms are arranged in open, airy clusters on upright stems from summer through frost. The rich, dark color is highly attractive to hummingbirds. As a compact perennial subshrub, it stays below 2-2.5 feet and is suitable for the front or middle of a sunny or partly shaded border in zones 8-10, or as a container specimen overwintered indoors in colder zones. Relatively rare compared to the common salvias but highly valued by gardeners who know it for its unusually large, darkly colored flowers.
